opp.ais_why:
• Across 1,605 chars of NextStep covering five dated entries from Feb to May 2026 ‚Äî including Horizon meeting with COO and CPO David Moran, business case build, tender wait ‚Äî zero mention of AI, Merlin, agentic or GenAI despite a heavy AI loadout (ML iAnalyze, Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI, ANA). Per the 12 May 2026 note: 'They will definitely be going to tender, however there has been a lot of resignations from senior management, so there has been some delay in getting budget approved. COO is championing this project internally and will get final sign off to start a tender in July.'
• Per the 22 Apr 2026 note: 'Met with three stakeholders including David the CPO at Horizon, Very interested in a demonstration for their executive team. Meeting on 5th May to run through suggested next steps.' On 10 Feb 2026: 'Send ANU access to the Zycus content portal/trust center... Include security pack: SOC/compliance, data residency, AU hosting (Macquarie DC), privacy/controls.' Buyer asks are security pack, AU hosting (Macquarie DC), implementation phasing, RFP template ‚Äî pure traditional procurement evaluation.
• Confidence is Medium. ANU has the second-broadest AI product loadout in chunk 3, an engaged COO sponsor, and CPO David Moran on personal connection with rep. But across 4 months of contact, no AI keyword has surfaced from the buyer side and the security/hosting concerns dominate. Score 5 (boundary DOWN per rubric) ‚Äî would flip to 7 only if the July tender requires AI/agentic capability.

champion.narrative: Simon Bernhard (Category Manager Cyber, Strategic Procurement Manager per contact role) is the primary champion, but his strength is developing and his engagement has cooled. On the 9 Feb 2026 call, Simon led the discovery session, detailed ANU's requirements, and committed to internal review of Zycus materials with Procurement, IT, and Security teams (action item: 'Review the Zycus portal content and shared materials within 24-48 hours'). He arranged a follow-up call (per action item: 'Schedule a one-hour follow-up session next week'), demonstrating active advocacy. On the 12 May 2026 call (93 days later), Simon remained engaged, discussed pricing favorably ('That's much more realistic with the budgets that we're having compared to other people, and I think you probably know who I'm talking about'), confirmed intent to tender ('They will definitely be going to tender' per Next Step 12 May), and committed to notify Zycus when the RFP is published on Tenderlink (action item). However, Simon has not replied to three rep follow-ups since then: 11 Jun (updates on COO/Chancellor approval), 18 Jun (status check, business case offer), and 30 Jun (Luke initiated contact). Last buyer engagement was 6 May incoming email (56 days ago per server pulse). Simon does not have access to the Economic Buyer (CFO) or authority to unstall the CFO/Chancellor budget approval (per Next Step 12 May). He has not introduced Zycus to David Moran (Head of Procurement, Decision Maker per 9 Feb call) or the CFO. Champion strength is weakening due to 56-day silence and lack of executive access.

competition.narrative: Coupa and SAP Ariba are the known competitors, with Coupa positioned as the primary threat but flagged as too expensive, and Ariba as a possible fallback. On the 9 Feb 2026 call, Simon was asked 'Why don't you use Ariba?' and 'Would you be evaluating Ariba in this instance?' (questions from Zycus rep per notes). Simon's response is not captured verbatim in the notes, but the questions imply Ariba is a known option. On the 12 May 2026 call, Simon stated (per note): 'Coupa can do, but they've got a very small team, and typically, all they do is they go through partners,' implying Coupa's implementation model is a concern. More critically, Simon said (per note): 'That's much more realistic with the budgets that we're having compared to other people, and I think you probably know who I'm talking about. You know what I mean?' and 'That's your 5 year cost is probably closer to their one to two year cost,' clearly referring to Coupa as overpriced relative to the relevant stakeholder suggests Coupa is a known peer but may be fading on cost grounds. SAP Ariba is listed in Salesforce Competitors__c field. However, Simon emphasized on 12 May that ANU is running a 'full open market tender… emphasizing best-of-breed solutions judged on capability,' so other vendors may enter. Zycus is positioned favorably on price and capability fit ('key stakeholders leaning to Zycus' per Next Step 8 Apr, 'very interested' per Next Step 22 Apr). The wedge is full-suite P2P with embedded AI/Merlin at government-affordable pricing, vs Coupa's high cost and partner dependency, and Ariba's ERP-native limitations.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is operational inefficiency, compliance risk, and vendor security exposure, owned by Procurement (David Moran, Head of Procurement) and championed by IT/Security (Simon Bernhard, Category Manager Cyber). On the 9 Feb 2026 call, Simon described current state: 'Word and Excel templates, siloed tender-to-contract practices, DocuSign memos and paper delegations, resulting in only 10% of 3,600 suppliers actively managed.' This creates tail-spend leakage, manual work, and governance gaps. ANU requires 'embedded AI workflows to capture tail spend' and 'governance stop-gates across requisitions' to enforce Commonwealth procurement compliance (9 Feb call). On the 12 May 2026 call, Simon emphasized reputational and security risk: 'recent leadership resignations and a Canvas LMS breach underscored the need for robust vendor security and reputation safeguards.' The Canvas breach 'forced system shutdown, disrupting teaching' with '200 days of degraded functionality, highlighting major security risk.' Pain owners are Simon (championing the project, per Next Step 12 May: 'COO is championing this project internally') and David Moran (Head of Procurement, per 9 Feb call). The CFO and Vice Chancellor approved funding for design and business case phases (9 Feb call), confirming pain is executive-acknowledged, but the quantified cost of the pain (savings, ROI) has not been built into a business case (per Next Step 18 Jun offer to assist).

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is a formal open-market government tender (RFQ/RFT), with approval chain CFO → Chancellor/Vice Chancellor, and evaluation by multi-stakeholder committee. On the 9 Feb 2026 call, Simon confirmed 'open market RFQ for a new P2P solution is planned for June or July, with design and business case phases already funded and executive-sponsored at the CFO and vice chancellor levels.' The 12 May call reiterated 'full open market tender planned for July/August.' Tender will be published on Tenderlink (government portal, per 12 May action item). Evaluation involves Procurement (David Moran, Head of Procurement), IT, Security Architecture, Enterprise Architecture (iTS teams per 9 Feb call), and end-user groups. Final approval requires CFO and Chancellor sign-off (per Next Step 12 May: 'COO is championing this project internally and will get final sign off to start a tender in July'). However, the process has stalled: the RFP was due June/July 2026 (now 1+ month overdue), budget approval is blocked at CFO/Chancellor level, and Chancellor Julie Bishop resigned 10 Jun 2026 (per Next Step 11 Jun news link), triggering mandatory board elections and governance instability. Until the RFP is published and budget cleared, the decision process cannot advance.

decision_criteria.narrative: Evaluation criteria are detailed and on record from the 9 Feb 2026 discovery call. ANU's RFP will judge vendors on: (1) government-compliant P2P platform ('Commonwealth procurement rules, embedding compliance gates that block non-compliant bids'), (2) Oracle ERP integration ('independent, government-compliant P2P platform integrated with the relevant stakeholder'), (3) embedded AI workflows to capture tail spend and reduce manual work, (4) governance stop-gates across requisitions/approval thresholds ('stopgates'/workflow controls, thresholds, PO–invoice match, reporting' per Next Step 10 Feb), (5) security and data residency (SOC compliance, AU hosting in Macquarie data center, privacy/controls per Next Step 10 Feb and 12 May emphasis on vendor security post-Canvas breach), (6) modular licensing and phased implementation options (big-bang 3–6 months vs phased 6–8 weeks per module, e-marketplace as phase 1, per 9 Feb call), and (7) best-of-breed capability over ERP-native modules (Simon stated on 12 May 'full open market tender planned for July/August, emphasizing best-of-breed solutions judged on capability'). Criteria are buyer-confirmed and documented, but the RFP has not been released so formal scoring has not begun.

blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Differentiated where it counts — ANU is AI Curious. The 9 Feb call emphasized 'embedded AI workflows to capture tail spend' as a core requirement, and the 12 May call noted 'University developing an AI strategy while balancing security concerns and managing unauthorized AI usage across campus.' However, AI appetite has not translated to executive urgency or a quantified AI-driven ROI case. ANU is exploring AI but has not made it a forcing function for the project. Zycus Merlin positioning is reactive (responding to Simon's questions about AI costs) rather than proactive (leading with AI as the wedge vs Coupa).
bullets.text: Differentiated where it counts — ANU is AI Curious.

bullets.full: Champion is driving it — Champion strength is weak and at risk. Simon Bernhard (Category Manager Cyber, Strategic Procurement Manager) is the sole active champion. He led the 9 Feb discovery call, gave favorable pricing feedback on 12 May, and committed to notify Zycus when the RFP is published. However, Simon has not replied to three rep follow-ups (11 Jun, 18 Jun, 30 Jun) over 56 days, and last buyer engagement was 6 May (56 days ago per server pulse). Simon lacks authority to unstall CFO/Chancellor budget approval and has not introduced Zycus to the CFO or Decision Maker (David Moran). No second champion exists. Champion is cooling during the leadership crisis and governance instability.

bullets.full: Buyer is leaning elsewhere — AI positioning is strong on paper (full product suite includes Merlin, Merlin Intake, Agentic AI per Products field; ANU explicitly requested 'embedded AI workflows to capture tail spend' on 9 Feb call and is 'developing an AI strategy' per 12 May call) but under-positioned in practice because the Merlin/AI narrative has not been elevated to CFO or executive level to differentiate vs Coupa. Simon asked on 12 May 'are there additional costs for the AI components?' (implying AI is seen as an add-on, not the core wedge). The AI value case (tail-spend capture, workflow automation, compliance stop-gates powered by Merlin) must be quantified in the ROI model and presented to the CFO to justify the project and win vs Coupa.

items.detail: RFP/tender release delayed indefinitely from original June/July 2026 target due to senior management resignations (Chancellor, four council members, other leadership turnover per 12 May call) and stalled CFO/Chancellor budget approval. With 113 days to 21 Oct close date and no tender yet released, the close date is not credible without immediate RFP publication and fast-track evaluation. Governance instability and slow government pace ('typical government, we work slow' per Simon, 12 May) create high timeline risk.

CFO (Economic Buyer) and the relevant stakeholder (Executive Sponsor) have not been directly engaged by the relevant stakeholder approval is stalled at CFO/Chancellor level per Next Step 12 May and 18 Jun. Without executive-level engagement to drive urgency, the deal remains dependent on operational champion (Simon) who lacks authority to unstall the governance and budget process.

Chancellor Julie Bishop resigned 10 Jun 2026 (per news link in Next Step 11 Jun), four council members stepped down under political pressure, and mandatory board elections triggered per 12 May call. Leadership instability undermines project sponsorship and creates risk that the P2P procurement project is deprioritized or delayed further pending new governance stability.

Budget approval for the tender has been delayed due to senior management resignations and governance instability per Next Step 12 May. flags: Executive-level engagement gap: CFO (Economic Buyer) and the relevant stakeholder (Executive Sponsor) have not been directly engaged on any recorded call or logged activity. With Coupa and SAP Ariba confirmed competitors, Zycus should intensify pre-RFP value-add engagement: (1) share government/university customer case studies with quantified ROI (tail spend capture, compliance improvement, security/vendor-risk mitigation post-breach analogs); (2) conduct informal AI/Merlin capabilities briefing with Simon, David, and IT/Security leads to demonstrate embedded-AI differentiation and stopgates/workflow automation; (3) provide Oracle Fusion integration deep-dive and Macquarie DC/AU-hosting security validation to lock in technical fit.
